name.en: Day of the Dead Bread (Pan de Muerto)

description.en: Mexico's Day of the Dead loaf: a rich, orange-and-anise sweet bread crossed with dough 'bones' and a round knob standing in for a skull, brushed with butter and rolled in sugar. Baked for family altars and breakfast on November 1st and 2nd.
description.de: Mexikos Brot zum Tag der Toten: ein buttrig-süßer Hefeteig mit Orangen- und Anisaroma, gekreuzt mit Teig-'Knochen' und einer runden Kuppel als Schädel, mit Butter bestrichen und in Zucker gewälzt. Für Ahnenaltäre und Frühstück am 1./2. November.
description.es: El pan de la ofrenda: una masa dulce y mantecosa con aroma a naranja y anís, cruzada con 'huesitos' de pan y una bola central que representa la calavera, barnizada con mantequilla y rebozada en azúcar. Para altares y desayuno el 1 y 2 de noviembre.
description.fr: Le pain du Jour des Morts au Mexique : une brioche parfumée à l'orange et à l'anis, marquée d'« os » en pâte et d'un dôme figurant un crâne, badigeonnée de beurre puis roulée dans le sucre. Pour les autels familiaux et le petit-déjeuner du 1er-2 novembre.

origin.region: Central and southern Mexico, now nationwide
origin.note: Baked as an offering (ofrenda) for departed relatives during Día de Muertos as well as a treat for the living; the crossed 'bones' and central knob evoke the skulls and skeletons central to the holiday's imagery.

ingredients.note: 60 g in the sponge, the rest in the final dough
melted, for basting the baked loaves

yield.shape: two 20 cm round loaves, crossed bones and a skull knob

keeping.note: Keeps about 3 days well wrapped at room temperature thanks to all the butter; the sugared crust softens first, so it's best within a day or two.

summary.en: Stir warm milk with a little yeast, sugar and flour and let it sit until visibly bubbling, about 20-25 minutes.
Beat in the remaining flour, salt, sugar, eggs, orange zest, anise and orange blossom water, then knead until the dough slaps the bowl; work in the butter gradually until glossy and smooth.
Let the dough roughly double at room temperature, then refrigerate it covered overnight to firm up and deepen in flavor.
Let the chilled dough warm up, then divide it: shape two large rounds for the loaves, and from the rest roll thin ropes pinched at intervals to look like bones, plus small balls for the skull knobs.
Cross two bone-shaped ropes over each round, anchor a ball in the center, then let the loaves proof, covered, until puffy, about an hour to 90 minutes.
Bake until lightly browned, then tent with foil and continue until deeply golden and cooked through, about 40-45 minutes total.
While still warm, brush the loaves generously with melted butter section by section and coat immediately in sugar before it cools.

source.adaptation: formula.* covers the mixed dough only; the basting butter and finishing sugar coat are listed as separate ingredients, not counted in fatPct/sugarPct.
